Nazarov hung on the neck of his horse, which careered round his comrades.  the horse under Ignatov ell, crushing his leg, and two of the mountaineers, without dismounting, drew their swords and hacked at his head and arms.  Petrakov was about to rush to his comrade's rescue when two shots -- one in his back and the other in his side -- stung him, and he fell from his horse like a sack.

Mishkin turned round and galloped off towards the fortress.  Khanefi and Bata rushed after him, but he was already too far away and they could not catch him.  When they saw that they could not overtake him they returned to the others.

Petrakov lay on his back, his stomach ripped open, his young face turned to the sky, and while dying he gasped for breath like a fish.

Gamzalo having finished off Ignatov with his sword, gave a cut to Nazarov too and threw him from his horse.  Bata took their cartridge-pouches from the slain.  Khanefi wished to take Nazarov's horse, but Hadji Murad called out to him to leave it, and dashed forward along the road.  His murids galloped after him, driving away Nazarov's horse that tried to follow them.  they were already among rice-fields more than six miles from Nukha when a shot was fired from the tower of that place to give the alarm.

 

* * *

 

"O good Lord!  O God! my God!  What have they done?" cried the commander of the fort seizing his head with his hands when he heard of Hadji Murad's escape.  "They've done for me!  They've let him escape, the villains!" cried he, listening to Mishkin's account.

An alarm was raised everywhere and not only the Cossacks of the place were sent after the fugitives but also all the militia that could be mustered from the pro-Russian aouls.  A thousand rubles reward was offered for the capture of Hadji Murad alive or dead, and two hours after he and his followers had escaped from the Cossacks more than two hundred mounted men were following the officer in charge at a gallop to find and capture the runaways.

After riding some miles along the high road Hadji Murad checked his panting horse, which, wet with sweat, had turned from white to grey.

To the right of the road could be seen the saklyas and minarets of the aoul Benerdzhik, on the left lay some fields, and beyond them the river.  Although the way to the mountains lay to the right, Hadji Murad turned to the left, in the opposite direction, assuming that his pursuers would be sure to go to the right, while he, abandoning the road, would cross the Al

To the right of the road could be seen the saklyas and minarets of the aoul Benerdzhik, on the left lay some fields, and beyond them the river.  Although the way to the mountains lay to the right, Hadji Murad turned to the left, in the opposite direction, assuming that his pursuers would be sure to go to the right, while he, abandoning the road, would cross the Alazan and come out onto the high road on the other side, where no one would expect him -- ride along it to the forest, and then after recrossing the river make his way to the mountains.

Having come to this conclusion he turned to the left; but it proved impossible to reach the river.  The rice-field which had to be crossed had just been flooded, as is always done in spring, and had become a bog in which the horses's legs sank above their pasterns.  Hadji Murad and his henchmen rode now to the left, now to the right, hoping to find drier ground; but the field they were in had been equally flooded all over and was now saturated with water.  The horses drew their feet out of the sticky mud into which they sank, with a pop like that of a cork drawn from a bottle, and stopped, panting, after every few steps.  They struggled in this way so long that it began to grow dusk and they had still not reached the river.  To their left lay a patch of higher ground overgrown with shrubs and Hadji Murad decided to ride in among these clumps and remain there till night to rest their exhausted horses and let them graze.  The men themselves at some bread and cheese they had brought with them.  At last night came on and the moon that had been shining at first, hid behind the hill and it became dark.  There were a great many nightingales in that neighborhood and there were two of them in these shrubs.  As long as Hadji Murad and his men were making a noise among the bushes the nightingales had been silent, but when they became still the birds again began to call to one another and to sing.

Hadji Murad, awake to all the sounds of night, listened to them involuntarily, and their trills reminded him of the song about Hamzad which he had heard the night before when he went to  get water.  He might now at any moment find himself in the position in which Hamzad had been.  He fancied that it would be so, and suddenly his soul became serious.  He spread out his burka and performed his ablutions, and scarcely had he finished before a sound was heard approaching their shelter.  It was the sound of many horses' feet splashing t

He might now at any moment find himself in the position in which Hamzad had been.  He fancied that it would be so, and suddenly his soul became serious.  He spread out his burka and performed his ablutions, and scarcely had he finished before a sound was heard approaching their shelter.  It was the sound of many horses' feet splashing through the bog.

The keen-sighted Bata ran out to one edge of the clump, and peering through the darkness saw black shadows, which were men on foot and on horseback.  Khanefi discerned a similar crowd on the other side.  It was Karganov, the military commander of the district, with his militia.

"Well, then, we shall fight like Hamzad," thought Hadji Murad.

When the alarm was given, Karganov with a troop of militiamen and Cossacks had rushed off in pursuit of Hadji Murad, but had been unable to find any trace of him.  He had already lost hope and was returning home when, towards evening, he met an old man and asked him if he had seen any horsemen about.  The old man replied that he had.  He had seen six horsemen floundering in the rice-field, and then had seen them enter the clump where he himself was getting wood.  Karganov turned back, taking the old man with him, and seeing the hobbled horses he made sure that Hadji Murad was there.  In the night he surrounded the clump and waited till morning to take Hadji Murad alive or dead.

Having understood that he was surrounded, and having discovered an old ditch among the shrubs, Hadji Murad decided to entrench himself in it and to resist as long as strength and ammunition lasted.  He told his comrades this, and ordered them to throw up a bank in front of the ditch, and his henchmen at once se to work to cut down branches, dig up the earth with their daggers, and make an entrenchment.  Hadji Murad himself worked with them.

As soon as it began to grow light the commander of the militia troop rode up to a clump and shouted:

"Hey!  Hadji Murad, surrender!  We are many and you are few!"

In reply came the report of a rifle, a cloudlet of smoke rose from the ditch and a bullet hit the militiaman's horse, which staggered under him and began to fall.  The rifles of the militiamen who stood at the outskirts of the clump of shrubs began cracking in their turn, and their bullets whistled and hummed, cutting off leaves and twigs and striking the embankment, but not the men entrenched behind it.  Only Gamzalo's horse, that had strayed from the others, was hit in the head by a bullet.  I

Only Gamzalo's horse, that had strayed from the others, was hit in the head by a bullet.  It did not fall, but breaking its hobbles and rushing among the bushes it ran to the other horses, pressing close to them and watering the young grass with its blood.  Hadji Murad and his men fired only when any of the militiamen came forward, and rarely missed their aim.  Three militiamen were wounded, and the others, far from making up their minds to rush the entrenchment, retreated farther and farther back, only firing from a distance and at random.

So it continued for more than an hour.  The sun had risen to about half the height of the trees, and Hadji Murad was already thinking of leaping on his horse and trying to make his way to the river, when the shouts were heard of many men who had just arrived.  These were Hadji Aga of Mekhtuli with his followers.  There were about two hundred of them.  Hadji Aga had once been Hadji Murad's kunak and had lived with him in the mountains, but he had afterwards gone over to the Russians.  With him was Akhmet Khan, the son of Hadji Murad's old enemy.

Like Karganov, Hadji Aga began by calling to Hadji Murad to surrender, and Hadji Murad answered as before with a shot.

"Swords out, my men!" cried Hadji Aga, drawing his own; and a hundred voices were raised by men who rushed shrieking in among the shrubs.

The militiamen ran in among the shrubs, but from behind the entrenchment came the crack of one shot after another.  Some three men fell, and the attackers stopped at the outskirts to the clump and also began firing.  As they fired they gradually approached the entrenchment, running across from behind one shrub to another.  Some succeeded in getting across, others fell under the bullets of Hadji Murad or of his men.  Hadji Murad fired without missing; Gamzalo too rarely wasted a shot, and shrieked with joy every time he saw that his bullet had hit its aim.  Khan Mahoma sat at the edge of the ditch singing "Il lyakha il Allakh!" and fired leisurely, but often missed.  Eldar's whole body trembled with impatience to rush dagger in hand at the enemy, and he fired often and at random, constantly looking round at Hadji Murad and stretching out beyond the entrenchment.  The shaggy Khanefi, with his sleeves rolled up, did the duty of a servant even here.  He loaded the guns which Hadji Murad and Khan Mahoma passed to him, carefully driving home with a ramrod the bullets wrapped in greasy rags, and pouring dry powder out of the pow

Eldar's whole body trembled with impatience to rush dagger in hand at the enemy, and he fired often and at random, constantly looking round at Hadji Murad and stretching out beyond the entrenchment.  The shaggy Khanefi, with his sleeves rolled up, did the duty of a servant even here.  He loaded the guns which Hadji Murad and Khan Mahoma passed to him, carefully driving home with a ramrod the bullets wrapped in greasy rags, and pouring dry powder out of the powder flask onto the pans.  Bata did not remain in the ditch as the others did, but kept running to the horses, driving them away to a safer place and shrieking incessantly, fired without using a prop for his gun.  He was the first to be wounded.  A bullet entered his neck and he sat down spitting blood and swearing.  Then Hadji Murad was wounded, the bullet piercing his shoulder.  He tore some cotton wool from the lining of his beshmet, plugged the wound with it, and went on firing.

"Let us fly at them with our swords!" said Eldar for the third time, and he looked out from behind the bank of earth ready to rush at the enemy; but at that instant a bullet struck him and he reeled and fell backwards onto Hadji Murad's leg.  Hadji Murad glanced at him.  His eyes, beautiful like those of a ram, gazed intently and seriously at Hadji Murad.  His mouth, the upper lip pouting like a child's, twitched without opening.  Hadji Murad drew his leg away from under him and continued firing.

Khanefi bent over the dead Eldar and began taking the unused ammunition out of the cartridge cases of his coat.

Khan Mahoma meanwhile continued to sing, loading leisurely and firing.  The enemy ran from shrub to shrub, hallooing and shrieking and drawing ever nearer and nearer.

Another bullet hit Hadji Murad in the left side.  He lay down in the ditch and again pulled some cotton wool out of his beshmet and plugged the wound.  This wound in the side was fatal and he felt that he was dying.  Memories and pictures succeeded one another with extraordinary rapidity in his imagination.  now he saw the powerful Abu Nutsal Khan, dagger in hand and holding up his severed cheek as he rushed at his foe; then he saw the weak, bloodless old Vorontsov with his cunning white face, and heard his soft voice; then he saw his son Yusuf, his wife Sofiat, and then the pale, red-bearded face of his enemy Shamil with its half-closed eyes.  All these images passed through his mind without evoking any feeling within him -- neither pity nor anger nor

All these images passed through his mind without evoking any feeling within him -- neither pity nor anger nor any kind of desire:  everything seemed so insignificant in comparison with what was beginning, or had already begun, within him.

Yet his strong body continued the thing that he had commenced.  Gathering together his last strength he rose from behind the bank, fired his pistol at a man who was just running towards him, and hit him.  The man fell.  Then Hadji Murad got quite out of the ditch, and limping heavily went dagger in hand straight at the foe.

Some shots cracked and he reeled and fell.  Several militiamen with triumphant shrieks rushed towards the fallen body.  But the body that seemed to be dead suddenly moved.  First the uncovered, bleeding, shaven head rose; then the body with hands holding to the trunk of a tree.  He seemed so terrible, that those who were running towards him stopped short.  But suddenly a shudder passed through him, he staggered away from the tree and fell on his face, stretched out at full length like a thistle that had been mown down, and he moved no more.

He did not move, but still he felt.

When Hadji Aga, who was the first to reach him, struck him on the head with a large dagger, it seemed to Hadji Murad that someone was striking him with a hammer and he could not understand who was doing it or why.  That was his last consciousness of any connection with his body.  He felt nothing more and his enemies kicked and hacked at what had no longer anything in common with him.

Hadji Aga placed his foot on the back of the corpse and with two blows cut off the head, and carefully -- not to soil his shoes with blood -- rolled it away with his foot.  Crimson blood spurted from the arteries of the neck, and black blood flowed from the head, soaking the grass.

Karganov and Hadji Aga and Akhmet Khan and all the militiamen gathered together -- like sportsmen round a slaughtered animal -- near the bodies of Hadji Murad and his men (Khanefi, Khan Mahoma, and Gamzalo they bound), and amid the powder-smoke which hung over the bushes they triumphed in their victory.

the nightingales, that had hushed their songs while the firing lasted, now started their trills once more:  first one quite close, then others in the distance.

 



 

It was of this death that I was reminded by the crushed thistle in the midst of the ploughed field.

the nightingales, that had hushed their songs while the firing lasted, now started their trills once more:  first one quite close, then others in the distance.

 



 

It was of this death that I was reminded by the crushed thistle in the midst of the ploughed field.

Author-Narrator.name: Author-Narrator
Author-Narrator.initial_description: First-person narrator walking home through fields in midsummer, gathering wildflowers, reflecting on nature's beauty and resistance

Hadji Murad.name: Hadji Murad
Hadji Murad.initial_description: Shamil's naib, famous for his exploits, never rode out without his banner. A Chechen warrior chief entering the hostile aoul of Makhmet on a cold November evening in 1851.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_003: Shamil's former naib, famous warrior and leader, now a fugitive riding with only one murid, cloaked in hood and burka with rifle, limping slightly, alert eyes scanning faces — a man of commanding presence reduced to hiding.

Hadji Murad's Murid.name: Hadji Murad's Murid
Hadji Murad's Murid.initial_description: A single strong, active murid accompanying Hadji Murad in flight; unnamed, acts as bodyguard and groom.

Old Man of Saklya.name: Old Man of Saklya
Old Man of Saklya.initial_description: Elderly Chechen man resting on a rooftop, toothless, wearing greasy beshmet and nightcap; recognizes Hadji Murad and receives him hospitably.

Boy of the Saklya.name: Boy of the Saklya
Boy of the Saklya.initial_description: Fifteen-year-old boy with black eyes 'like ripe sloes'; son of the host, sent to fetch his father from the mosque.

Woman of the Saklya.name: Woman of the Saklya
Woman of the Saklya.initial_description: Slight, spare, middle-aged woman in yellow smock, red beshmet, blue trousers; domestic host figure, arranges cushions for Hadji Murad.

Sado's Mother.name: Sado's Mother
Sado's Mother.initial_description: Slight, spare, middle-aged Chechen woman in yellow smock, red beshmet, and wide blue trousers; Sado's household woman who arranges cushions for the guest

Eldar.name: Eldar
Eldar.initial_description: Hadji Murad's murid — strong legs, fine eyes (described as cut off mid-sentence); silent, loyal companion who mirrors his leader's every ritual action
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_005: Hadji Murad's murid (devoted follower); young, silent, with fine ram-like eyes; sits cross-legged and observes the old man's talk.
Chunk chunk_008: One of Hadji Murad's murids; follows his leader's cue to stop eating, shows disciplined loyalty.
Chunk chunk_025: Young Lesghian murid, broad-shouldered with a slim waist, beautiful ram-like eyes, and a beginning brown beard. Physically striking.

Sado's Father.name: Sado's Father
Sado's Father.initial_description: Old man at the saklya; lifeless red eyes; keeps to the apiary; speaks in veiled proverbs about Russian raids; refuses to say directly what he knows

Sado.name: Sado
Sado.initial_description: Master of the house (saklya), about forty, small beard, long nose, black eyes; devout and hospitable; bound by kunak (guest-friendship) code; warns HM of Shamil's arrest order.

Sado's Son.name: Sado's Son
Sado's Son.initial_description: Fifteen-year-old boy, nimble, black-eyed; runs errands for his father.

Bata.name: Bata
Bata.initial_description: Sinewy, short-legged Chechen guide, deeply sun-burnt, tattered yellow Circassian coat; cheerful and confident; will escort Eldar to the Russians.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_006: Sinewy, short-legged Chechen, sun-darkened, wearing a worn yellow Circassian coat and crumpled black leggings; a skilled guide boasting no Chechen can pass where he can; loyal to Hadji Murad for honor, not money.
Chunk chunk_011: Shorter Chechen man who approaches Russian sentry post at night, declares himself friendly, unarmed, and acting as a messenger for a prince
Chunk chunk_012: Shorter Chechen scout; arrives at Russian outpost at night seeking Prince Vorontsov, speaks broken Russian, unarmed and diplomatic
Chunk chunk_019: Referenced as having already departed with Khan Mahoma; messenger/liaison role
Chunk chunk_020: Accompanies Khan Mahoma on the mission to the Russian prince; appears alongside him as a messenger/guide figure

Khan Mahoma.name: Khan Mahoma
Khan Mahoma.initial_description: One of Hadji Murad's four horsemen waiting at the Argun River bend; designated envoy to be escorted to Prince Vorontsov by Bata; knows what to do and say.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_019: Departed with Bata before Hadji Murad's arrival; messenger/envoy figure
Chunk chunk_020: One of Hadji Murad's murids, sent on a mission to the Russian prince; returns with news that the prince received him warmly

Sado's wife.name: Sado's wife
Sado's wife.initial_description: Thin middle-aged Chechen woman, Sado's wife; serves guests and arranged the cushions; carries domestic hospitality role.

Sado's daughter.name: Sado's daughter
Sado's daughter.initial_description: Young Chechen girl; wears red trousers and green beshmet; silver-coin necklace and a silver ruble at her plait; sloe-black eyes bright in a face trying to be stern; aware of visitors but does not look at them.

Sado's Wife.name: Sado's Wife
Sado's Wife.initial_description: Unnamed Chechen woman, brings food and tea to the guests; wears coin ornaments that tinkle as she moves; deferential, silent, efficient in the saklya

Sado's Daughter.name: Sado's Daughter
Sado's Daughter.initial_description: Young girl with sloe-black eyes, tries to look stern; carries basin, ewer, and towel; does not look at visitors but is aware of them

Sado's Boy.name: Sado's Boy
Sado's Boy.initial_description: Young son who sits by the door gazing at Hadji Murad's face with sparkling eyes, smiling in confirmation of his father's words of welcome

Panov.name: Panov
Panov.initial_description: Non-commissioned officer leading a night ambush patrol of three soldiers from Vozvizhensk fort beyond the Shahgirinsk Gate.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_009: Non-commissioned officer on night ambush duty; pragmatic, leads soldiers in casual camp behavior; addresses Avdeev familiarly
Chunk chunk_010: Russian soldier, calm and pragmatic; mediates disputes within the company; pipe smoker on night watch.

Avdeev.name: Avdeev
Avdeev.initial_description: Russian soldier; cheerful, resourceful; improvises a ground-pipe for smoking; earns Panov's praise as 'smart chap'
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_010: Russian soldier, cheerful but plagued by existential dullness; admits to drinking away his sadness; confides openly about despair.

Nikitin.name: Nikitin
Nikitin.initial_description: Russian soldier, morose and dissatisfied; wants the company commander held accountable for borrowing from the chest.

Ukrainian Soldier.name: Ukrainian Soldier
Ukrainian Soldier.initial_description: Third unnamed soldier; makes a dry joke about Nikitin's crooked mouth; minor voice in the night-watch scene.

Bondarenko.name: Bondarenko
Bondarenko.initial_description: Russian soldier on night watch with Avdeev and Panov; minor background presence, about to recount a dream when interrupted

Simon Vorontsov.name: Simon Vorontsov
Simon Vorontsov.initial_description: Prince Simon Mikhailovich Vorontsov, Commander of the Kurin Regiment, Imperial Aide-de-Camp, son of the Commander-in-Chief; identified as the 'Prince' the scouts seek
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_013: Prince, Commander of the Kurin Regiment, Imperial Aide-de-Camp, son of the Commander-in-Chief; long-faced, wearing insignia and gold cords

Marya Vasilevna.name: Marya Vasilevna
Marya Vasilevna.initial_description: Princess Vorontsova, Simon's wife, famous Petersburg beauty; large-built, large-eyed, black-browed; exercises magnetic social power over men around her
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_026: Princess at Vorontsov's house; mentioned at chunk's end as Vorontsov arrives home with Hadji Murad
Chunk chunk_027: Princess, Vorontsov's wife; elegantly dressed, socially adept, charmed by Hadji Murad's flush when she extended her hand
Chunk chunk_028: Wife of Prince Vorontsov; speaks French with her husband; observes and translates Hadji Murad's gestures socially; acts as social lubricant in the reception scene.
Chunk chunk_045: Vorontsov's wife, the 'Sirdar's wife'; hosts the evening party, goes about half-dressed among visitors, exchanges pleasantries with Hadji Murad through interpreter

Poltoratsky.name: Poltoratsky
Poltoratsky.initial_description: Company commander, broad red-faced man who exchanged out of the Guards; infatuated with Marya Vasilevna to the point of distraction during card play
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_021: Russian officer commanding two companies of woodcutters beyond the Shagirinsk Gate; formerly at Cadet College with Baron Freze

Regimental Adjutant.name: Regimental Adjutant
Regimental Adjutant.initial_description: Officer sitting straight with cold expression on handsome face; partners against Vorontsov; flushes with irritation at Poltoratsky's blunders

Petersburg Tutor.name: Petersburg Tutor
Petersburg Tutor.initial_description: Shaggy young man of gloomy appearance, graduate of Petersburg University; sent to Caucasus by Princess Vorontsova to tutor her son from first marriage

Adjutant.name: Adjutant
Adjutant.initial_description: Prince Vorontsov's adjutant, white-ringed hands, deals cards, pleased when princess joins game against him

Tikhonov.name: Tikhonov
Tikhonov.initial_description: Poltoratsky's comrade sharing his bedroom; wakes briefly to ask about cards and Marya Vasilevna
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_016: Poltoratsky's orderly or companion, reminds him they must start at six, speaks practically
Chunk chunk_021: Poltoratsky's subaltern, present at the woodcutting operation in the Shalin glade

Vovilo.name: Vovilo
Vovilo.initial_description: Poltoratsky's domestic serf, over forty, taken into household service as a boy; drunk on return; broods over his unfree life and the illusory promise of emancipation after the Caucasus campaign
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_016: Poltoratsky's personal servant, takes his boots and clothes, protests about waking him if he fights

Ivan Petrovich.name: Ivan Petrovich
Ivan Petrovich.initial_description: Ordnance sergeant, off-screen; Vovilo's envy-object — has a salary, is married, expects discharge

Khanefi.name: Khanefi
Khanefi.initial_description: Hadji Murad's sworn brother who manages his household affairs; first to take hold of Hadji Murad's bridle and stirrup upon arrival at the forest camp
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_020: One of Hadji Murad's murids who carries equipment (ewer) in the group's packs; logistical support figure
Chunk chunk_025: Thick-set, hairy Avar murid with meeting eyebrows; manages all of Hadji Murad's property and leads the pack-horse with saddle bags.

Chunk chunk_054: Hadji Murad's fourth murid, an Avar from the same aoul as HM. Hairy face and neck, arched moss-like chest; strong, hardworking, quietly obedient. Became HM's blood-brother after a traditional ritual (no shaving or nail-cutting for two months, then nursing from HM's mother Patimat) to escape a blood feud — his father killed HM's uncle.

Gamzalo.name: Gamzalo
Gamzalo.initial_description: A Chechen murid; silently takes up a rifle and stations himself on guard without being asked when Hadji Murad warns of pursuit
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_025: Chechen murid, short red beard, no eyebrows or lashes, blind in one eye, scar across nose and face. Menacing presence.

Baron Freze.name: Baron Freze
Baron Freze.initial_description: Ex-officer of the Guards, reduced to the ranks for dueling; fellow cadet-college classmate of Poltoratsky; present at the Shalin glade encampment

Freze.name: Freze
Freze.initial_description: Russian officer, Poltoratsky's comrade; addressed as 'Kostya', sent back to his company when shots are fired
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_023: Russian officer (Baron Freze per graph context), jumps up and moves quickly toward his smoke-covered company position; minor field officer.

Bulka.name: Bulka
Bulka.initial_description: Vorontsov's curly-haired, keen-eyed stepson, age six; fascinated by Hadji Murad's warrior reputation; briefly sits on Hadji Murad's knee
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_028: Young son of Vorontsov and Marya Vasilevna; admires Hadji Murad's dagger and receives it as a gift per Chechen kunak custom; sits on Hadji Murad's knee.

General Kozlovsky.name: General Kozlovsky
General Kozlovsky.initial_description: Commander of the left flank at Grozny; named recipient of Vorontsov's formal report of Hadji Murad's defection

Meller-Zakomelsky's Aide-de-Camp.name: Meller-Zakomelsky's Aide-de-Camp
Meller-Zakomelsky's Aide-de-Camp.initial_description: Military aide who delivers the general's demand that Hadji Murad be presented immediately; functionally anonymous messenger.

Meller-Zakomelsky's wife.name: Meller-Zakomelsky's wife
Meller-Zakomelsky's wife.initial_description: Model-looking little lady, wife of Baron Meller-Zakomelsky, present during the confrontation but silent.

Doctor (Fort Hospital).name: Doctor (Fort Hospital)
Doctor (Fort Hospital).initial_description: Military doctor who examines Avdeev's wound and notices old scars on his back

Typhus Patient.name: Typhus Patient
Typhus Patient.initial_description: Soldier with high fever, one of four patients already in the hospital ward

Ague Patient.name: Ague Patient
Ague Patient.initial_description: Pale soldier with dark shadows under his eyes, expecting a fever attack, yawning continuously

Hand-Wound Soldier.name: Hand-Wound Soldier
Hand-Wound Soldier.initial_description: Soldier wounded in the hand during a raid three weeks prior; up and about

Shoulder-Wound Soldier.name: Shoulder-Wound Soldier
Shoulder-Wound Soldier.initial_description: Soldier wounded in the shoulder during a raid three weeks prior; sitting on a bed

Avdeev's Father.name: Avdeev's Father
Avdeev's Father.initial_description: Old peasant farmer, wakes before dawn to prepare threshing floor; stoic and hardworking
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_034: Old peasant man, patriarchal head of threshing household, bitterly compares Akim unfavorably to the absent Peter/Avdeev
Chunk chunk_035: Frugal old man who reluctantly agrees to send Peter one ruble from oat-sale proceeds; regrets having let Peter go

Avdeev's Sister-in-Law.name: Avdeev's Sister-in-Law
Avdeev's Sister-in-Law.initial_description: Wife of the brother in whose stead Peter enlisted; works the threshing floor

Avdeev's Niece.name: Avdeev's Niece
Avdeev's Niece.initial_description: Daughter of Avdeev's brother, nearly marriageable age; works alongside family

Akim.name: Akim
Akim.initial_description: Eldest son of the peasant family, scolded by father for laziness and drinking; he attends to horses before joining threshing

Avdeev's Mother.name: Avdeev's Mother
Avdeev's Mother.initial_description: Old woman who brings news of the Elder's work order, sides with father in scolding Akim
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_035: Old woman who mourns Peter's absence and persuades her husband to send him money; dictated the letter to the church clerk
Chunk chunk_036: Peter's mother, who dictates the farewell letter through tears; receives the returned letter with news of Peter's death and weeps, then has a requiem said for him.

Avdeev's Daughter-in-law.name: Avdeev's Daughter-in-law
Avdeev's Daughter-in-law.initial_description: Turns oats with flail; tries briefly to defuse father-son quarrel

Akim Avdeev.name: Akim Avdeev
Akim Avdeev.initial_description: Peter Avdeev's elder brother, stays home while Peter was conscripted; accused by mother of being Peter's inferior

Akim's Wife (daughter-in-law).name: Akim's Wife (daughter-in-law)
Akim's Wife (daughter-in-law).initial_description: Attempts to mediate between mother-in-law and Akim with 'let bygones be bygones'

Avdeev's Father (Old Man).name: Avdeev's Father (Old Man)
Avdeev's Father (Old Man).initial_description: Peter Avdeev's elderly father, a peasant who drives the oat sledges to town and has the letter read to him at the inn.

Avdeev's Grandson.name: Avdeev's Grandson
Avdeev's Grandson.initial_description: The old man's grandson who drives the last sledge to town.

Aksinya.name: Aksinya
Aksinya.initial_description: Peter Avdeev's widow, who had left the family home and gone into service; laments loudly upon hearing of his death.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_037: Peter Avdeev's widow; publicly mourns her husband while privately relieved — she is pregnant by a shopman she lives with and hopes to marry.

Shopman (Aksinya's lover).name: Shopman (Aksinya's lover)
Shopman (Aksinya's lover).initial_description: Unnamed merchant who has promised to marry Aksinya once she is free; father of her unborn child.

Vanka.name: Vanka
Vanka.initial_description: Aksinya's infant son from her marriage to Peter Avdeev; mentioned as the orphaned child she laments.

Michael Vorontsov.name: Michael Vorontsov
Michael Vorontsov.initial_description: Russian Viceroy of the Caucasus, 70+; English-educated, ambitious, charming aristocrat who wields imperial power with elegant ease. Conqueror of Napoleon at Krasnoe. Receives the news of Hadji Murad's surrender.

Elizabeth Ksaverevna Vorontsova.name: Elizabeth Ksaverevna Vorontsova
Elizabeth Ksaverevna Vorontsova.initial_description: Princess, wife of Michael Vorontsov; hostess of the Tiflis palace dinner.

Princess Manana Orbelyani.name: Princess Manana Orbelyani
Princess Manana Orbelyani.initial_description: Tall, fine, handsome woman of Oriental type, about forty-five years of age; seated beside Vorontsov at dinner.

Georgian Princess (Vorontsova's friend).name: Georgian Princess (Vorontsova's friend)
Georgian Princess (Vorontsova's friend).initial_description: Graceful, dark, red-cheeked Georgian woman glittering with jewels, incessantly smiling; seated at Vorontsov's left.

Countess Choiseuil.name: Countess Choiseuil
Countess Choiseuil.initial_description: Princess Vorontsova's friend, escorted to dinner by a Georgian prince.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_040: Elderly French-speaking friend of Princess Vorontsova; reacts with horror ('Quelle horreur!') to the Hadji Murad widow story.

Doctor Andreevsky.name: Doctor Andreevsky
Doctor Andreevsky.initial_description: Aide-de-camp at the Tiflis dinner, follows first couples in to table.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_041: Card-game partner of Vorontsov; described as remarkable for the great influence he exercised

Carroty General.name: Carroty General
Carroty General.initial_description: Red-haired visiting general with bristly mustaches; seated to the right of Princess Vorontsova; has met Hadji Murad more than once.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_039: Unnamed Russian general with bristly mustaches, seated near the princess at Vorontsov's dinner; tells anecdotes about Hadji Murad without social awareness, inadvertently embarrassing Vorontsov by invoking the Dargo debacle.

Manana Orbelyani.name: Manana Orbelyani
Manana Orbelyani.initial_description: A woman present at the dinner who reads the social danger of the general's story and intervenes with a deflecting question to protect Vorontsov's dignity.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_040: Princess at the Tiflis dinner table who interrupts the general's boastful story by asking about his quarters, tactfully steering the conversation.
Chunk chunk_041: Georgian noblewoman at the dinner who flatters Vorontsov in French

Georgian Prince.name: Georgian Prince
Georgian Prince.initial_description: A very stupid but extraordinarily refined and artful flatterer and courtier sitting beside Princess Vorontsova; saves the social situation by redirecting conversation to Hadji Murad anecdotes.

Armenian Guest.name: Armenian Guest
Armenian Guest.initial_description: Anonymous Armenian who had been in Temir Khan Shura in 1849 and relates the particulars of Hadji Murad's daylight raid on the shops.

Armenian General.name: Armenian General
Armenian General.initial_description: An Armenian guest who learned ombre from Vorontsov's valet; present at the card table

Bristly-Mustached General.name: Bristly-Mustached General
Bristly-Mustached General.initial_description: Yesterday's general in full uniform with all his decorations, present in the waiting room to take his leave of Vorontsov.

Court-Martialled Regiment Commander.name: Court-Martialled Regiment Commander
Court-Martialled Regiment Commander.initial_description: Commander of a regiment in danger of court martial for misappropriating commissariat money; waiting on Vorontsov.

Rich Armenian Vodka Merchant.name: Rich Armenian Vodka Merchant
Rich Armenian Vodka Merchant.initial_description: Wealthy Armenian patronized by Doctor Andreevsky, seeking government renewal of his vodka sale monopoly.

Officer's Widow.name: Officer's Widow
Officer's Widow.initial_description: Widow of a killed officer, dressed in black, seeking pension or free education for her children.

Ruined Georgian Prince.name: Ruined Georgian Prince
Ruined Georgian Prince.initial_description: Dispossessed Georgian noble in magnificent Georgian costume seeking confiscated Church property.

Official with Caucasus Plan.name: Official with Caucasus Plan
Official with Caucasus Plan.initial_description: An official bearing a large roll of paper containing a new plan for subjugating the Caucasus.

Visiting Khan.name: Visiting Khan
Visiting Khan.initial_description: A Khan who came solely to be able to say he had called on the prince; purely social.

Fair-Haired Aide-de-Camp.name: Fair-Haired Aide-de-Camp
Fair-Haired Aide-de-Camp.initial_description: Handsome young aide-de-camp who shows petitioners in and out of Vorontsov's cabinet.

Loris-Melikov.name: Loris-Melikov
Loris-Melikov.initial_description: Vorontsov's aide-de-camp, assigned to accompany and supervise Hadji Murad in Tiflis
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_059: Russian officer receiving Hadji Murad's dictated life story; interlocutor and listener in this confession scene

Osman.name: Osman
Osman.initial_description: Hadji Murad's eldest brother, born first; the event of his birth prompted their mother to nurse the eldest Khan.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_055: Hadji Murad's brother who participated in the mosque assassination of Hamzad; killed by Hamzad's murids during the attack

Abu Nutsal Khan.name: Abu Nutsal Khan
Abu Nutsal Khan.initial_description: Eldest son of the Khan of Khunzakh; nursed by Hadji Murad's mother before HM was born.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_048: One of three young Khans; foster-brother of Hadji Murad's brother Osman; mentioned as part of the Khansha's court in Hadji Murad's childhood memory.

Chunk chunk_050: Eldest son of the Khansha; proud and easily shamed into dangerous action — the Khansha calls him afraid and he rides to Hamzad despite Hadji Murad's warning; headed toward the massacre.

Umma Khan.name: Umma Khan
Umma Khan.initial_description: Second son of the Khan of Khunzakh; also nursed and reared by Hadji Murad's mother.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_048: One of three young Khans; Hadji Murad's sworn brother from the Khansha's palace; part of childhood memory.
Chunk chunk_049: Second son of the old Khansha; sent with Hadji Murad to Tiflis to seek Russian help; physically strong and brave but weak-willed, corrupted by Russian officers
Chunk chunk_050: Second son of the Khansha, slow of speech; brought by Hadji Murad to Hamzad's camp as the first of the Avar Khan hostages; passive and unable to resist Hamzad's manipulation.

Bulach Khan.name: Bulach Khan
Bulach Khan.initial_description: Youngest son of the Khansha; born around the same time as Hadji Murad — the mother refused to nurse him, precipitating the father's violent attack.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_048: Youngest of the three Khans; Shamil later threw him over a precipice; referenced in Hadji Murad's backstory.
Chunk chunk_049: Youngest son of the Khansha; sent as hostage to Hamzad per his demand; received well by Hamzad initially
Chunk chunk_050: Youngest son of the Khansha; sent first as hostage to Hamzad, received well — used to lure the older brothers.

Hadji Murad's Mother.name: Hadji Murad's Mother
Hadji Murad's Mother.initial_description: Courageous Avar woman; nursed two Khan sons, refused a third time to protect her own child, was stabbed by her husband, survived, and composed a lament-song about the wound.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_048: Sung of in a lullaby/war song; bore a wound in her side; carried Hadji Murad as a child, now held captive by Shamil. Central emotional anchor.

Hadji Murad's Father.name: Hadji Murad's Father
Hadji Murad's Father.initial_description: Passionate, violent man who stabbed his wife for refusing to wet-nurse again; stopped by bystanders.

Kazi-Mulla.name: Kazi-Mulla
Kazi-Mulla.initial_description: Former Imam before Hamzad; killed before Hadji Murad's story begins; contextualizes the rise of Muridism.

Baron Rosen.name: Baron Rosen
Baron Rosen.initial_description: Russian Commander-in-Chief at Tiflis who refused to receive Hadji Murad and Umma Khan, offered verbal help but did nothing

Khansha.name: Khansha
Khansha.initial_description: Old widow-ruler of Khunzakh; sent Hadji Murad and Umma Khan to Tiflis to seek Russian protection against Hamzad; later sent Bulach Khan as hostage
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_050: The widowed Avar ruler-mother; described as weak, conceited, and easily manipulated; her vanity triggers the deaths of her sons by sending them to Hamzad.

Aseldar.name: Aseldar
Aseldar.initial_description: Hamzad's favorite murid who killed the Khansha and was in turn killed by Hadji Murad in the mosque ambush

Osman (brother).name: Osman (brother)
Osman (brother).initial_description: Hadji Murad's brother, killed during the revenge killing of Hamzad's murderers; died fighting alongside Hadji Murad

Sultanetta.name: Sultanetta
Sultanetta.initial_description: Khansha's daughter; refused marriage to Akhmet Khan's son, triggering Akhmet Khan's vendetta against Hadji Murad

General Rosen.name: General Rosen
General Rosen.initial_description: Russian general who granted Hadji Murad an officer's commission and authority to govern Avaria

lines.line: Selaam aleikum, old father. May thy sons live.
Ne habar?
lines.subtext: He wants to know what the old man will hide.

lines.line: The night is cold. A horse and one murid — no more.
My murid. Eldar is his name. He eats what thou givest.
Thy chimney is fresh. A good house stands quiet tonight.
lines.subtext: He has noticed who else may be watching.

lines.line: I do not ride with a banner now. The road chooses the man.
lines.subtext: Admits fall from naib without self-pity.

lines.line: Shamil eats where he eats. I do not speak of him under thy roof.
lines.subtext: Signals break, refuses to curse host's allegiance.

lines.line: Tell me only this — is the path below the wood still open?
lines.subtext: Road stands in for family, escape, life.

lines.line: If Allah wills, we ride before the moon stands over the saklyas.
My son Yusuf is in Shamil's hand. A father hears that in his sleep.
I ask no bread I cannot repay. A saddle, water, silence.
Inshallah, the dogs will sleep tonight. Wake us before the muezzin.
lines.subtext: Testing whether old man will wake only them.

chunk_003_tension_0_55.notes: Lines 4-14 words; Ne habar probes; Shamil not cursed in host's house.

lines.line: Selaam aleikum, old father. The night rode ahead of us.
May thy sons live. Thy roof still stands well.
lines.subtext: Testing whether Shamil's men have been.

lines.line: My murid. Eldar is his name. He sleeps where I sleep.
The horse can stand here tonight. No further.
Ne habar?
lines.subtext: He has read the boy's startled eyes.

lines.line: Sado prays late. Call him, but quietly — no lamp at the door.
May thy coming bring happiness also to this house, sister.
The rifle beside thy master's. The sword also. It is fitting.
lines.subtext: Placing his fate inside household's honor.

lines.line: I will sit. Eldar stands. So it must be tonight.
lines.subtext: Refusing full hospitality warns pursuers close.

lines.line: Of Shamil — not in this house. Tell me only of the road from the north.
lines.subtext: Wants to hear which pass is open.

lines.line: Inshallah, before the second prayer we are gone.

chunk_004_tension_0_55.notes: Composure holds; every courtesy a probe.

lines.line: Selaam aleikum. Thy roof still stands. That is much, in such a season.
lines.subtext: Reading old man's face for what he won't say.

lines.line: Ne habar?
Thy son knows. Good. A son who knows is a roof of his own.
Hares and eagles. Yes. The Mitchit hay will grow again, old father.
lines.subtext: Will not echo curse in another man's saklya.

lines.line: My murid. Eldar is his name.
He will eat from thy hand as I do. His dagger sleeps where mine sleeps.
The horse outside — is there water behind the saklya, or must we go to the stream?
A short night is enough. Before the moon climbs over the ridge, we are gone.
lines.subtext: Warning host not to plan long stay.

lines.line: Tell me only this, old father: is the road to the upper aoul still open to men on foot?
May thy sons live. And the woman at the hearth — may her hands never tire.
lines.subtext: Politeness scaled up because trust scaled down.

lines.line: Eldar. The saddles stay close. The rifles on that nail — we know them by touch.
Inshallah, by the second prayer we are in the wood.

chunk_005_tension_0_55.notes: Fugitive composure; courtesy intensifies with suspicion.

fusion_candidates.reason: The unnamed murid riding with HM in this chunk serves the same escort/loyal-companion function as Eldar; if this is not Eldar, the two roles are functionally identical — single companion to HM in flight. CF-01 applies: <5 scenes, shared ally function.

Old man is a threshold greeter with zero independent arc; his hospitality function is identical to Sado's kunak role. In compression he can be dissolved into Sado's arrival greeting, eliminating one unnamed figure → CF-01, CF-02
Both are unnamed Chechen wives performing hospitality gestures (cushions/greeting); functionally interchangeable supporting roles, same thematic echo → CF-01, CF-02
Both are young male witnesses to Hadji Murad in a domestic Chechen setting; the wide-eyed boy watching the legend arrive is the same beat as Sado's son. One witness-boy is sufficient → CF-01
The unnamed murid in earlier chunks is now revealed to be Eldar — same character, now named; no fusion needed, just canonical name unification to 'Eldar'

cuttable.element: Detailed botanical catalogue of flowers (clover, daisies, rape blossoms, campanulas, vetch, scabious, plaintains, cornflowers, dodder)
cuttable.reason: Pure literary texture — non-load-bearing world-colour with no protagonist connection. In film, replace with a single wide shot of the field. → SS-03, SC-01

cuttable.element: Narrator's physical struggle to pluck the thistle (handkerchief, five-minute battle, fraying stalk)
cuttable.reason: Can be compressed to a single visual beat — hands reaching, thistle resisting. The extended prose description is non-filmable as written. → SC-01, SC-06

cuttable.element: Narrator's philosophical reflection on man as destructive creature (ploughed field meditation)
cuttable.reason: Pure authorial digression; no character action, no dramatic weight, no protagonist involvement. Non-filmable as written. → SS-04, SC-01 (fails all three tests: no protagonist-drive, minimal thematic load beyond metaphor already carried by the thistle image itself, information fully replaceable by visual)

cuttable.element: Narrator's authorial frame ('I had partly seen myself, partly heard of from eye-witnesses, and in part imagined')
cuttable.reason: Pure NF/SS-04 auktorial digression; no dramatic action, no character agency. Equivalent of Tolstoy stepping in front of the story. CUT per NF-04 and SS-04 — the film opens on Hadji Murad's action, not the narrator's epistemological caveat.

cuttable.element: Old Host (unnamed) as separate character
cuttable.reason: Pure threshold-greeter; zero scenes beyond this arrival moment; his hospitality function absorbed by Sado → CF-01, SS-03

cuttable.element: Host's Wife (unnamed) as separate character
cuttable.reason: Single-beat hospitality gesture; interchangeable with Sado's Wife already in cast; non-load-bearing → CF-01, SS-03

cuttable.element: Detailed description of old man's dressing ritual (slippers, sheepskin, ladder descent)
cuttable.reason: Atmospheric Tolstoian prose detail; filmable as montage background but expendable in compression without losing plot beat → SC-01, SS-04

cuttable.element: Mitchit aoul hay-burning (off-screen reference)
cuttable.reason: Non-load-bearing contextual detail; Russian scorched-earth already established by broader raid context — SS-03, SC-01

refocalize.scene: Entire prologue frame (thistle discovery and reflection)
refocalize.current_pov: Authorial I (Tolstoy's own first-person narrator, outside the story)
refocalize.recommended_pov: TRANSLATE_TO_VISUAL — open on extreme close-up of the crimson thistle standing upright in a ditch; cut to a hand (anonymous, gloved — Russian) reaching for it; struggle, fraying, discard. No voice-over. The image speaks the theme. Optionally: brief title card with the thematic line ('With what determination it defended itself...') as the only textual intrusion, then dissolve into the Caucasus story. Alternatively, dissolve the frame entirely and embed the thistle as a recurring visual motif (HM's cloak, the Chechen embroidery) per NF-04.

refocalize.scene: Thistle discovery and reflection on the black-earth field
refocalize.current_pov: Authorial Narrator (first-person observer outside the story world)
refocalize.recommended_pov: VISUAL — camera as silent observer; open on extreme close-up of the broken thistle on black earth, no narrator present. Cut to title or direct match-cut into Caucasian episode. Per NF-04: dissolve narrator voice; per SC-06: translate inner experience into image.

refocalize.scene: Narrator's philosophical reflection on the thistle and the Caucasian episode ('What vitality! … I remembered …')
refocalize.current_pov: Authorial I — omniscient, retrospective, explicit moral commentary
refocalize.recommended_pov: TRANSLATE_TO_VISUAL: open on a close-up of the thistle crushed by the wheel, still upright — cut directly to Hadji Murad riding alone into Makhmet. The thistle image speaks without the narrator. CF-08 + NF-04.

refocalize.scene: Exposition of Hadji Murad's former status as banner-bearing naib with dozens of murids
refocalize.current_pov: Omniscient narrator telling the audience what HM used to be
refocalize.recommended_pov: REFOCAL via visual contrast: a brief flash (memory or montage) of HM with his full retinue, then hard-cut to the present lone rider. Alternatively, a reaction shot from a villager who recognises him but says nothing — the gap between former glory and present secrecy told through other eyes. NF-09, CF-08.

refocalize.scene: Arrival at saklya — old man on roof waking
refocalize.current_pov: Omniscient authorial narrator (Tolstoy observes the old man's physical details from outside)
refocalize.recommended_pov: Hadji Murad's POV — we see only what he sees arriving: the sleeping figure, the ritual greeting, the domestic interior. Cuts authorial description, retains visual beat → CF-08, NF-04

refocalize.scene: Boy's sparkling eyes fixed on new arrivals
refocalize.current_pov: Authorial narrator noting the boy's appearance
refocalize.recommended_pov: HM's gaze — he clocks the boy, reads the household; brief shot/reverse shot. The boy's wonder mirrors the audience's. → CF-08

refocalize.scene: Old man's allegorical intelligence briefing
refocalize.current_pov: Third-person omniscient narrator, with slight old-man interiority ('did not wish to say')
refocalize.recommended_pov: Hadji Murad's POV exclusively — we read his face reading the old man; what he infers is shown through micro-reaction, not narration

direct_quotes: But what energy and tenacity!  With what det
But what energy and tenacity!  With what determination it defended itself, and how dearly it sold its life!
Ah, what a destructive creature is man....How many different plant-lives he destroys to support his own existence!
Man has conquered everything and destroyed millions of plants, yet this one won't submit.
What vitality! Man has conquered everything and destroyed millions of plants, yet this one won't submit.

Setting established: midsummer, hay harvest finished, rye reaping beginning — rural Russia
Narrator introduces the central symbolic motif: the crimson 'Tartar' thistle, which resists destruction and admires energy/tenacity — the frame device that will link to Hadji Murad
Narrator demonstrates a reflective, aesthetic, moralizing sensibility
Chunk cuts off mid-exclamation, introducing the thematic keywords 'energy and tenacity'

Anchors the coming narrative in late 1851 and in the Caucasus

plot_elements.description: Author-Narrator gathers wildflowers in a midsummer field during harvest season, observing the variety and beauty of blooms

Author-Narrator's reflection on human destructiveness and the thistle's indomitable vitality — 'Man has conquered everything and destroyed millions of plants, yet this one won't submit.' — pivots into recall of a Caucasian episode.
Narrative frame pivot: Author-Narrator introduces the Caucasian episode as partly witnessed, partly heard, partly imagined — setting November 1851 as temporal anchor for the main story.
Hadji Murad stops at a specific saklya, wakes the old man with a whip-handle tap, and is recognized — implies prior relationship and a network of safe houses.
The woman of the house receives Hadji Murad with the traditional hospitality greeting and begins preparing cushions — Chechen guest-right (kunak culture) is activated.
Eldar introduced as Hadji Murad's murid — mirrors leader's disarming ritual, seated beside him; establishes the murid group as a unit
Hadji Murad decides to send a letter to the Russians via his murid Eldar; Sado volunteers his brother Bata as guide — the defection-to-Russians is set in motion.

Hadji Murad retrieves a secret note from his cartridge-pouch and entrusts it to Sado to forward to his son — first explicit signal that he is working to communicate with his family trapped under Shamil

Chapter II begins: POV shifts to Vozvizhensk fort; Panov leads three Russian soldiers to a night ambush position at the blackened plane tree — establishes the Russian military presence surrounding the same geography Hadji Murad occupies.
Soldiers gossip about company commander dipping into the cashbox after card losses; Panov defers to collective company decision
Company commander has borrowed from the communal chest again; soldiers debate whether to demand an accounting — Nikitin insists, Panov and Avdeev counsel patience.
Interior of Vorontsov's house at midnight: lavish card game while all other barracks are dark — the imperial outpost lives in two worlds simultaneously
Marya Vasilevna's proximity reduces Poltoratsky to distraction, costing him aces and angering the adjutant — flirtation as social currency in the fort
Poltoratsky finds his door locked; scolds the drunk Vovilo, stops short of striking him
Vovilo's interior monologue: compares his lot to Ivan Petrovich's, despairs over his serf condition and the hollow promise of freedom post-Caucasus
Poltoratsky retires to bed, daydreaming about Marya Vasilevna; orders Vovilo to wake him at five for the morning expedition
Hadji Murad decides immediately to flee; Sado confirms horses are already saddled — showing Sado anticipated the danger.
Hadji Murad daydreams/fantasizes about defeating Shamil with Russian army support, ruling all of Chechnya — reveals his hidden ambition and transactional view of defection

Two Russian companies under Poltoratsky are felling trees beyond the Shagirinsk Gate in the Shalin glade; officers (Poltoratsky, Tikhonov, Baron Freze and two others) eat, drink vodka and porter on drums while awaiting events
Russian officers drink vodka and porter on drums after an operation, discussing the romanticised death of General Sleptsov; ironic gap between war mythology and reality is explicit
Poltoratsky mounts and leads his company toward the incoming fire, relishing the prospect of combat
Poltoratsky mounts and leads his company toward firing on the ravine outskirts; Chechen pursuers of Hadji Murad exchange shots with the Russian line.
Vorontsov reflects on jurisdictional tension with General Meller-Zakomelsky — he bypassed the chain of command and fears repercussions even amid triumph
Alone in his room, Hadji Murad drops his public mask: anxiety replaces courtesy. He checks on murids' arms, horses, and accommodations via Eldar.
Vorontsov's watch chimes; Marya Vasilevna prompts her husband to give it as a gift; Hadji Murad accepts with visible delight — a diplomatic gesture that softens the surface tension.
Marya Vasilevna insists on accompanying the men to Meller-Zakomelsky despite her husband's objections, asserting personal agency inside Russian military politics.
Simultaneous with Avdeev's death, his family threshes oats on the farm — domestic life indifferent to his sacrifice
Aksinya, Peter's widow, also learns of his death and laments, mourning both husband and her own ruined life.
Aksinya performs public grief for Peter Avdeev while privately rejoicing — his death frees her to marry the shopman who fathered her second child. Hypocrisy under social pressure is laid bare.
Vorontsov dismisses the jurisdictional quarrel between Simon and Meller-Zakomelsky — 'Simon a eu tort. But all's well that ends well'
Vorontsov reads Simon's letter about Hadji Murad's surrender and Simon's clash with Meller-Zakomelsky; dismisses Simon's fault with characteristic patrician ease ('All's well that ends well').
Hadji Murad reveals his family are hostages of Shamil — he will not openly act against Shamil until they are exchanged for Russian prisoners; this is the central constraint on his cooperation

Hadji Murad appears at the Vorontsovs' Monday evening party; Russian social world (half-dressed women, champagne, band) is rendered alien through his impassive gaze — culture-clash contrast established
Chapter XI opens: on the fifth day Loris-Melikov arrives by Vorontsov's command to formally record Hadji Murad's life story for transmission to the Tsar — institutional memoir-extraction begins.
Hadji Murad begins his oral autobiography: birth in Tselmess, family's closeness to the Khunzakh Khans through wet-nursing.

Hadji Murad recites his mother's war song — a lullaby of survival through bloodshed — and declares she is now Shamil's captive and must be rescued. This fuses the personal (maternal love) with the political (the real motive for his defection to the Russians).

Hadji Murad's detailed childhood memory: the fountain, head-shaving, the brass mirror, his grandfather's silverwork, his mother's lepeshki. These form his psychological baseline — what he is fighting to recover.
Hadji Murad recalls palace life with the three Khans (Abu Nutsal, Umma, Bulach), his position of privilege, and wealth — the world before Muridism disrupted everything.
The arrival of Murid missionaries ('beat stones with wooden scimitars, cried Ghazavat') triggers mass conversion of Chechens and Avars — the historical tide that will sweep Hadji Murad into the war.

Kazi-Mulla is killed, Hamzad succeeds him, and Hamzad sends envoys demanding the Khans join the Ghazavat — the political pressure that leads directly to the assassinations Hadji Murad will narrate next.
Hadji Murad recounts his early prosperity and pleasure-seeking life before the Ghazavat
Hamzad threatens the Khans: join the Ghazavat or face destruction of Khunzakh
Baron Rosen refuses audience; Russian officers corrupt Umma Khan with wine, women, and card games — he loses everything
Khansha sends Bulach Khan as hostage to Hamzad; Hamzad invites the two elder brothers to follow
Hamzad sends a sheik offering Ghazavat instruction in exchange for the Khansha's youngest son as hostage — a calculated first step to lure all three Avar Khans.
Shots fired in Hamzad's tent — the massacre of the Avar Khans begins (off-screen within this chunk, mid-sentence cut).
Khan Mahoma smokes secretly in defiance of Hadji Murad's prohibition; Gamzalo rebukes him and leaves — establishing Gamzalo as the strict loyalist and Khan Mahoma as the irreverent pragmatist.
Khan Mahoma recounts gambling in Tiflis — winning by bluff with only 12 kopecks; reveals willingness to kill rather than lose, illustrating his reckless vitality

Loris-Melikov suspects Hadji Murad's surrender may be a spy mission — Gamzalo's open hatred of Russians reinforces the suspicion. Russian intelligence anxiety about HM's true motives surfaces explicitly.
HM summons Loris-Melikov back to the drawing room to resume narrating his life story (Chapter XIII opens). Storytelling-as-diplomacy thread continues.
Hadji Murad continues dictating his life story to Loris-Melikov in the drawing room — the flashback narrative frame resumes
Hamzad murders the Khansha on a murid's sword after taking the palace — completing the annihilation of the Avar Khan family
Hadji Murad and his brother Osman devise a blood-revenge ambush against Hamzad after consulting their grandfather; the plot is overheard and Hamzad warns the grandfather
Mosque assassination: Hadji Murad kills Aseldar with a dagger, Osman shoots Hamzad, Hadji Murad delivers the killing blow — Osman dies in the ensuing fight while Hadji Murad fights off thirty murids
HM escapes on the Mansokha path: jumps off 120-yard abyss, drags a soldier with him; soldier dies, HM survives — the legendary escape
Hadji Murad recounts his escape at Mansokha: tied, jumps into a 120-yard abyss pulling a soldier with him, survives with multiple broken bones, found by a shepherd.
Hadji Murad shows Loris-Melikov two letters from General Klugenau — first letter summons him to prove innocence of treason charges brought by Akhmet Khan.
Hadji Murad summarizes his written reply: turban worn for spiritual reasons, cannot join Shamil (who killed his kin), cannot join Russians (dishonored by them — a bound man spat on him).
Hadji Murad regains leadership over the Avars after recovering at Tselmess — confirms his political restoration and the power vacuum he filled.
Hadji Murad hands Loris-Melikov Klugenau's letter urging him to defect to the Russians, citing the dishonor done to him as the obstacle; he reads Klugenau's pledge of Russian justice.
Hadji Murad explains why he did not trust Klugenau and did not go over: his primary motive was revenge on Akhmet Khan, which the Russians could not deliver.
Akhmet Khan besieges Tselmess and attempts to capture or kill Hadji Murad; Hadji Murad has too few men to resist.

Shamil sends an envoy with a letter promising to help Hadji Murad destroy Akhmet Khan and naming him ruler of Avaria; after long deliberation, Hadji Murad joins Shamil and begins fighting the Russians.
Hadji Murad narrates his many military exploits to Loris-Melikov — a retrospective summary of campaigns already partly known to Loris-Melikov.
Hadji Murad concludes his life story to Loris-Melikov: Shamil's broken promise, the dismissal from Naib, seizure of property, and forced defection to Vorontsov — family still held hostage by Shamil
Hadji Murad explicitly states his constraint: 'I am tied up and the end of the rope is in Shamil's hands' — family hostage situation named as the central dramatic obstacle
Vorontsov writes to Chernyshov (20 December) in French, reporting Hadji Murad's arrival in Tiflis and his preoccupation with his family — Russian command evaluating Hadji Murad's utility
Hadji Murad reveals Shamil exploited him: sent him to Tabasaran, then stripped him of property and demanded money — betrayal finalizes HM's defection logic
Loris-Melikov reports Hadji Murad's arrival in Tiflis on the 8th and his week of conversations with him about his family's captivity under Shamil.
Hadji Murad is paralysed without news of his family — cannot serve the Russians effectively while they remain Shamil's hostages.
Hadji Murad requests a prisoner exchange plus ransom money to free his family; Loris-Melikov lacks legal authority to provide funds.
Loris-Melikov warns Hadji Murad frankly: Shamil will likely not release the family and may instead threaten to kill them to pressure his return.
Hadji Murad's defiant response: he will never surrender to Shamil, whom he believes will kill him regardless; his life is in God's hands.
Hadji Murad expresses desire to serve on the Lesghian line and pledges results within a month or accepts punishment.

themes: Aksinya mourns husband and self together — the war destroys domestic futures as well as lives
Anonymous soldiers die far from home while bureaucratic language ('defending his Tsar, his Fatherland, and the Orthodox Faith') sanitizes the loss
Anonymous soldiers die while the imperial machine erases and falsifies their deaths
Anonymous soldiers pay the price for imperial posturing — Avdeev wounded by casual, almost recreational firing ordered by Poltoratsky
Anonymous soldiers pay the price for imperial posturing — Avdeev's wound inflicted by casual military order, his death unremarked
Anticipated encounter between worlds — Hadji Murad's mountain order vs. Russian imperial military bureaucracy
Blood vengeance as sacred duty — 'where the forelegs have gone the hind legs must follow'
Body's refusal of comfort: Avdeev's gut rejects vodka — the body knows what the mind won't admit (mortal wound)
Bureaucratic anxiety beneath imperial triumph — Vorontsov's private doubt undercuts public victory
Bureaucratic betrayal: Klugenau's absence enables Akhmet Khan's persecution — institutional failure as personal doom
Bureaucratic deferral as a form of power — Vorontsov's 'I will think it over' traps Hadji Murad without refusing him
Bureaucratic delay vs. urgent personal stakes — Vorontsov's deliberate unhearing of the family-ransom plea shows imperial machinery indifferent to human urgency
Cheerful proximity to death — officers joke and drink while bullets fly
Chivalric code of the mountain warrior contrasted with drawing-room gossip
Civilisation as performance: the drawing room enacts imperial normalcy over an abyss of mutual suspicion
Civilizational suspicion masked by courtly ritual — the dinner scene externalises paranoia without a word of accusation
Class and patronage vs. rank and merit — family connections versus years of service
Class hierarchy enforced through casual violence (threat to strike, then withdrawal)
Comic relief as character exposition — Khan Mahoma's winking irreverence vs. Gamzalo's rigid piety
Complicit pursuers — the twenty men chase only to perform loyalty to Shamil, not from conviction
Compulsory serf labor (carting bricks) juxtaposed with compulsory military service: both systems grind the peasant body.
Confession as political negotiation — Hadji Murad's story is simultaneously personal truth and tactical positioning
Conscription as social death — 'a soldier was a severed branch'
Costume as autobiography — HM's turban carries the history of his persecution by Akhmet Khan and Klugenau, worn as silent defiance
Cross-cultural charm: HM's blush at Marya Vasilevna's hand — vulnerability beneath the warrior legend
Cultural incomprehension as unbridgeable wall — HM's polite non-answer to the half-naked women encapsulates the entire Russian-Chechen contact zone
Death as conversion: the dying murid's last words as turning point
Death with dignity versus war's indignity: Avdeev's grace contrasts with Poltoratsky's breezy 'how goes it, my lad'
Desire as distraction — Poltoratsky's infatuation mirrors the Russian military's blindness to real stakes
Dignity in surrender — Hadji Murad's bearing is imposing even in submission
Diplomatic contact as threshold moment — the defection process begins at night, clandestinely
Discipline and restraint as warrior identity — HM eats a mouthful after 24 hours of fasting
Distrust codified in alliance — two enemies bind themselves out of necessity
Domestic life continues indifferent to imperial sacrifice — the farm knows nothing of the war
Domestic life of the colonial officer class as counterpoint to HM's fugitive exhaustion
Dullness and existential drift as the soldier's true enemy
Eagles dwell in mountains — the murid's proud retort signals that honour survives defection
Empire as social theatre — Vorontsov's palace dinner trivializes HM's world-altering surrender to a courier dispatch
Faith and discipline as warrior identity — prayer maintained even in flight
Family as Hadji Murad's hidden wound — the note establishes his central vulnerability
Family as hostage — the personal cost of political defection made explicit
Family as political hostage — love weaponised by power
Family grief displaced as domestic anger — the cost of empire felt in the kitchen, not just the battlefield.
Family separation haunts even the fantasy — the dream includes Shamil's wives weeping, echoing HM's own captive family
Fate and chance in war: 'Each man get what fate sends'
Female social power operating outside formal military hierarchy
First impressions vs. legend — the fearsome warrior reveals a disarming human smile
First seeds of HM vs. Shamil antagonism planted here
Flattered vanity as political lever: Loris-Melikov instrumentalises HM's pride to extract information
Flight and pursuit as existential condition — Hadji Murad is perpetually hunted, never safe
Flight as assertion of will — HM chooses defection over submission even under fire
Flogging scars as palimpsest: the empire has always written violence on peasant bodies
Forgiveness and selflessness as the only freedom available to the powerless — Avdeev's dying message releases his brother from guilt
Gamzalo as uncaged threat — red eye fixed on horse ears, contempt coded in body language
Gender seclusion as lived cultural code — not commented on, simply enacted
Gilded cage: gifts (the watch) signal ownership, not generosity; HM checking the hour underscores his need to reclaim agency within captivity
God as the only court above geopolitics — HM's fatalism as dignity
HM's men as a mirror of HM: each murid a facet of his character (frivolous / iron / obedient / blood-bound)
HM's silent political intelligence — he reads power dynamics without language, through observation alone
Hadji Murad as legend vs. Hadji Murad as person — the gap between image and reality widens
Hadji Murad as pawn passed between officials, his agency suspended while Russians squabble
Hadji Murad as spectacle — his very presence in a room of petitioners signals how far he has fallen from independent warrior to supplicant
Hadji Murad as the one who sees the trap — his political intelligence established in backstory
Hadji Murad caught between two systems, both flawed
Hadji Murad surrounded yet purposeful — compressed danger breeds decisive action
Hadji Murad's agency curtailed step by step — from honoured guest to man in a waiting room
Hadji Murad's defection radiates outward: the Chechen pursuers' presence directly causes Avdeev's wound — HM's choice has mortal consequences for those around him
Hadji Murad's legend precedes him even into the drawing rooms of his enemies
Hadji Murad's legend precedes him; his fame silences a room before he appears
Half-naked Russian women as civilizational marker — Tolstoi's ironic ethnography of imperial mores
Hierarchy of loyalty: Eldar mirrors HM even in hunger, subordinating self entirely
Hierarchy of wakefulness: power stays lit while subordinates sleep
Historical tide as personal fate — Muridism's spread is not ideology but the tide that drowned his world
Honor as non-negotiable constraint — the spit incident explains everything
Hospitality as mortal risk — Sado's loyalty costs him; kunak bond tested under pressure
Hospitality as performance — kunak gift custom weaponizes generosity as diplomatic shield
Imperial bureaucracy as cage: Loris-Melikov wants to help but is legally constrained
Imperial bureaucracy as obstacle — chain of command subordinates the historic moment to petty ego
Imperial bureaucracy as theater — the waiting room stages the full spectrum of those who depend on Vorontsov's favor, underscoring power's corrupting reach
Imperial bureaucracy vs. warrior autonomy — Vorontsov's French letter reduces HM to an intelligence asset
Imperial bureaucracy: chain-of-command quarrel resolved by handshake while the subject of the quarrel sits excluded in the next room
Imperial chain-of-command friction: Vorontsov's personal diplomacy collides with Meller-Zakomelsky's institutional authority
Imperial condescension — French spoken as private channel excluding HM
Imperial court as myth-manufacturing machine — praise of the enemy serves the patron's vanity
Imperial masculinity and the performance of courage under fire
Imperial mercy as transactional currency, not moral virtue
Imperial myth-making machine: atrocities laundered into heroism at dinner tables
Imperial power as solemn ceremony: Vorontsov's 'stern and solemn' face contrasts yesterday's social warmth
Imperial privilege insulated from the violence it commands
Imperial self-deception: official history vs. lived truth — the Dargo campaign is a 'brilliant achievement' in reports, a catastrophe in memory
Imperial social performance masking military reality — champagne and cards while Hadji Murad waits outside
Imperial social performance vs. private reality — Poltoratsky's ecstasy dissolves in a grimy billet
Imperial social spectacle as alienation — the opera and ball render Hadji Murad permanently Other
Imperial society as performance — dinner as stage where power and information are displayed
Information asymmetry as power — Marya Vasilevna already knows, forces Vorontsov to confirm
Kunak hospitality as moral absolute — Sado risks death yet does not hesitate
Kunak-loyalty as moral counter-code to imperial hierarchy — Hadji Murad extends it sincerely, Russians receive it as exotic ceremony
Legend vs. human reality — the terrifying chief has a child's smile
Letters as instruments of power and persuasion — Klugenau's written promises fail where Shamil's practical offer succeeds
Loyalty and brotherhood — Osman dies at Hadji Murad's side
Loyalty as a transactional commodity — Shamil's betrayal mirrors the Russian system Hadji Murad now enters
Loyalty as spectrum: Eldar's absolute devotion vs. Khan Mahoma's transactional allegiance vs. Gamzalo's silent resistance
Loyalty defined by blood: HM refuses Shamil because Osman's and Abu Nutsal Khan's blood is on his hands
Loyalty forged through ritual versus imposed through hierarchy — Khanefi's milk-brotherhood vs Russian chain of command
Loyalty made visible in the body — Eldar's sleeping posture (cartridge-pouches still on) as emblem of warrior identity at rest
Loyalty without certainty — Gamzalo praises the master he abandoned, exposing the complexity of allegiance
Loyalty without words — Gamzalo and Eldar act without being commanded, embodying the murid ethos of total devoted service
Mansur as lost golden age: purity, communal virtue, divine favour vs. Shamil's politicised authority
Maternal sacrifice as the root of Hadji Murad's identity — his existence was purchased by his mother's blood
Memory as motivation — childhood images (fountain, brass mirror, lepeshki) make the family rescue emotionally concrete, not abstract
Mortality of the common soldier vs. spectacle of the great man — Avdeev fades as HM arrives
Mother as origin of courage — the war song transfers the wound from mother to son symbolically
Mutual deception as diplomatic language — both parties know the other is lying and perform sincerity anyway
Narrative framing: the story told in safety contrasts with the extreme violence of the events described
Night as the domain of the hunted — stars, darkness, the hum from the mosque
Night as threshold: the ordinary watch becomes the hinge of history
Object as diplomatic gesture: the watch gift is an exchange of trust tokens across an unbridgeable cultural divide
Officers' detachment from suffering: Poltoratsky's breezy 'Tut, tut, tut' against Avdeev's agony
Oral history / storytelling as power: HM's life will reach the Tsar through his own words
Paranoid vigilance beneath social grace — HM's private face vs. public face
Patrician blindness — Vorontsov's snuff-taking pleasure and dismissal of Simon's fault reveals his insulation from consequence
Peasant grief is practical and brief: weep, return to work, hold a requiem, distribute holy bread
Peasant stoicism and hidden grief: mother's mourning channelled into a single ruble
Personal honor and revenge as political driver — HM joins Shamil not for ideology but to settle a blood debt with Akhmet Khan
Physical strength vs. moral weakness (Umma Khan)
Political humiliation as weapon (Hamzad's treatment of the Elders)
Power asymmetry masked by ceremony: sitting protocol as status negotiation
Power dynamics of flattery: courtiers read the host and adjust their speech accordingly
Power maintained through elegance — Vorontsov's portrait shows how imperial domination is laundered through culture
Power performed through spectacle — Hamzad's cavalry display mirrors imperial theater seen in Russian Tiflis chapters
Power withheld as social currency — Vorontsov refuses to explain the officer's message
Prayer and weapon as twin anchors of identity under threat
Privilege lost — Hadji Murad's palace youth creates the contrast with his current gilded-cage captivity under Russia
Promised freedom deferred indefinitely — structural echo of HM's family hostage situation
Public performance vs. private truth — Aksinya's false grief mirrors the imperial court's performative propriety
Revenge as cycle-closer and new-cycle-opener: killing Hamzad ends one arc, immediately births Shamil's threat
Ritual preparation for surrender as performance of dignity
Romantic distraction as imperial disease — Poltoratsky preoccupied with Marya Vasilevna's hand rather than tomorrow's orders
Russian imperial hollow promises — power without honor
Russian officers' casual indulgence (vodka, porter, drums as seats) as contrast to Hadji Murad's disciplined self-presentation
Russian soldiers' double vision of Hadji Murad: admired dzhigit and feared killer simultaneously
Self-mythology and fortune: Hadji Murad's supreme confidence in his own destiny
Self-sacrifice without recognition — Avdeev's enlistment as act of love turned bitter
Serfdom as invisible cage — Vovilo's paralysis mirrors HM's political captivity
Shame as the crucible of courage — HM's one moment of flight becomes the source of lifelong fearlessness
Shamil as destroyer of family — Bulach Khan thrown over precipice foreshadows what Shamil may do to Hadji Murad's own family
Shamil as the invisible force constraining Hadji Murad's agency
Shamil's reach as invisible antagonist: decree of death shapes every decision
Shared humanity across enemy lines — Avdeev's easy fraternisation with Chechen scouts
